talk_metadata.talk_title: Dysregulated AKT Signaling Reprograms Osteosarcoma to Drive Selective Reliance on EP300
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we don't see major changes, but of course, you do see some changes" when discussing toxicity of p300 degraders in normal tissues
Speaker said: "there are lots of folks, including some in the audience, who have tried this before, and the answer is that it slows down tumor growth, but it doesn't actually involute the tumor"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: tumor cells with high translational capacity resist p300 degradation
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: we see that resistant lines have a higher translational rate

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: PROTAC targeting only p300, sparing CBP
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed that the degrader interacts with cereblon E3 receptor, suggesting reliance on a single E3 ligase
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Targeting a single paralog may allow tumor escape via CBP compensation

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that AKT dysregulated osteosarcoma is highly sensitive to p300 degradation, yet concluded that "it might be challenging" to develop a p300 inhibitor due to potential toxicity in normal developing tissues

key_data_gaps: No in vivo efficacy data in orthotopic pediatric models beyond two PDX lines
Lack of toxicity assessment in pediatric-relevant models

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify translational signatures that predict resistance to p300 degradation and combine with translation inhibitors
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "we think what's going on here is that we're using AKT inhibitors to actually slow down translation, and that's allowing JQuad to work better"
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model translation-rate biomarkers and predict patients who would benefit from combined AKT inhibitor + p300 degrader therapy

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a biomarker panel for AKT pathway activation (PTEN loss, AKT amplification) to select osteosarcoma patients for JQUAD1 therapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker identified that all sensitive lines had mutations hyperactivating AKT pathway
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate genomic and transcriptomic data to generate a predictive AKT‑activation score

unresolved_questions: What is the precise mechanism by which high translational capacity confers resistance to p300 degradation?
Can the p300 degrader be safely combined with AKT inhibitors in pediatric patients?

rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I must admit that this is preliminary data, and we're currently working on that" regarding resistance mechanisms
Speaker said: "we have a hint that that might occur with temozolomide treatment" about clonal hematopoiesis

vulnerability_identified.failing_compound_or_target: Current chemotherapy backbones (bevacizumab, dinutuximab beta) in BEACON trial
vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of biomarkers to predict response and monitor resistance
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: we don't really know the biomarkers of response
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: some patients achieved 30-35% response rates but overall outcomes remain poor

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: Neuroblastoma response to bevacizumab or anti‑GD2 dinutuximab beta combined with chemotherapy
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: Mixed relapsed and refractory patients without molecular stratification
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: Predictive ctDNA or mutation‑based biomarkers for response
trial_dilution_risk.static_vs_dynamic_failure: No serial ctDNA‑guided adaptation of therapy
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Factorial multi‑arm design may dilute signal for each backbone

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that high cfDNA quantity at enrollment predicts worse outcome, yet earlier they stated that cfDNA quantity did not correlate with disease burden (SIOPEN score)

key_data_gaps: No functional validation of identified resistance mutations
Limited analysis of GD2 synthesis pathway alterations

crispro_opportunity.description: Integrate cfDNA mutation and copy‑number signatures to stratify patients for anti‑GD2 therapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted that cfDNA quantity is prognostic but not linked to specific biomarkers
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can combine cfDNA genomic data with clinical variables to generate a risk model for therapy selection

crispro_opportunity.description: Target emergent RAS/MAPK and FGFR1 mutations that appear at relapse
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted enrichment of ALK, RAS/MAPK, and FGFR1 alterations in relapse samples
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict downstream pathway dependencies and suggest combination inhibitors

unresolved_questions: What are the functional consequences of the identified FGFR1 mutations?
Can early cfDNA dynamics be used to adapt therapy in real time?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Spatial T Cell Receptor Profiling Uncovers Barriers to T Cell Engagement and Shared Tumor-Reactive Clonotypes in High-Risk Neuroblastoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we don't know which antigen specificity of this clone is yet" regarding NY‑ESO‑1 prediction
Speaker said: "we are currently transitioning to Visium HD" indicating current platform limitations

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Downregulation of antigen‑presenting molecules limits alpha‑beta T cell engagement
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: alpha beta T cells are functionally constrained due to the downregulation of antigen‑presenting molecules
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: gamma delta T cells show higher infiltration and may represent a subset of responders

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed reliance on custom probe design, limiting rapid adaptation to new clonotypes
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Spatial profiling limited to pre‑selected 100 clonotypes, potentially missing relevant clones

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated that gamma delta T cells are enriched near neural blasts, yet concluded that "T‑cell transcriptional state is driven by location rather than clonotype identity alone" which downplays the functional relevance of the clonotype

key_data_gaps: No functional validation of predicted antigen specificity (e.g., NY‑ESO‑1)
Limited longitudinal sampling to assess clonotype dynamics over therapy

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine gamma delta T cell expansion agents with CAR constructs targeting identified shared clonotypes
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted gamma delta T cells as spatially privileged and sharing clonotype 2 across patients
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model gamma delta TCR repertoires and predict synergistic cytokine environments for combination therapy

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a spatial‑omics based biomarker to select patients with high gamma delta T cell infiltration for immunotherapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed that gamma delta T cells have the highest proportion of neural blast neighbors (45%)
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate spatial proximity metrics with transcriptomics to generate a predictive score

unresolved_questions: What are the antigen specificities of the shared gamma delta clonotypes?
Do gamma delta T cells retain functionality after tumor infiltration?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Co-targeting GPC2 and B7H3 with CAR T cells achieves durable responses in preclinical high-risk neuroblastoma and medulloblastoma models
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we did see that there is some value in doing bicistronic architecture over co-transduction"
Speaker said: "we hypothesized that the T cells could protect these mice from tumor rechallenge"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Tumor downregulation of GPC2 under CAR pressure
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: we see that GPC2 was downregulated in all groups
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: some mice achieved complete response with dual CARs

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Bicistronic CAR construct linking GPC2 and B7H3 scFvs
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted reliance on a specific CD3ζ omission strategy that may be patent‑encumbered
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Complex vector design may increase manufacturing variability
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Potential for antigen loss (GPC2) remains; B7H3 expression heterogeneity not fully characterized

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that B7H3 expression is stable across treatment but concluded that "bicistronic CARs are superior" without addressing possible B7H3 heterogeneity in patient tumors

key_data_gaps: Lack of data in immune‑competent models
No assessment of off‑target toxicity to normal tissues expressing B7H3

crispro_opportunity.description: Redevelop GPC2‑only CARs with CRISPR‑mediated up‑regulation of GPC2 to overcome antigen loss
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker reported GPC2 downregulation in all treatment groups
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can predict transcriptional regulators of GPC2 and suggest epigenetic drugs to sustain its expression

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine bicistronic GPC2/B7H3 CARs with MDSC‑targeting agents to address relapse linked to MDSC infiltration
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker observed high MDSC infiltration in relapsed bicistronic CAR‑treated mice
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model tumor‑immune microenvironment and prioritize agents that deplete MDSCs

cited_competitors.name: Dotti group
cited_competitors.context: demonstrated CD3ζ omission improves CAR fitness

unresolved_questions: Will bicistronic CARs retain efficacy in an immune‑competent setting?
What is the durability of B7H3 expression in patient tumors over time?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Surface-localized Nuclear Envelope Proteins Define Therapeutic Vulnerability in MYC-Driven Group 3 Medulloblastoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we think that it's almost like it gets lodged there and aberrantly stays there" about LBR retention
Speaker said: "we haven't gotten that far" regarding functional role of surface LBR

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Low surface density of each antigen limits CAR activation
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: dual LBR/TMPO CARs showed more durable remission

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Camelid single‑domain antibody‑based CARs against LBR/TMPO
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker disclosed extensive engineering of nanobodies, indicating a narrow IP space
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Low antigen expression may require higher CAR expression levels, complicating manufacturing
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Potential off‑target expression of nuclear envelope proteins in normal tissues under stress not fully evaluated

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed that LBR/TMPO are minimally expressed on the surface yet concluded that bispecific CARs achieve durable remission without addressing how low antigen density might affect safety

key_data_gaps: No toxicity assessment in normal pediatric brain tissue
Unclear mechanism of LBR surface mislocalization

crispro_opportunity.description: Leverage CrisPRO's subcellular proteomics modeling to predict other mislocalized nuclear envelope proteins as CAR targets
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized that conventional pipelines miss nuclear envelope proteins
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate proteomics and subcellular localization data to prioritize novel surface antigens

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a diagnostic assay for LBR surface expression to select Group 3 patients
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed LBR enrichment at recurrence and post‑therapy
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model expression dynamics and propose a cutoff for patient selection

unresolved_questions: Does surface LBR have a functional role in tumor signaling?
What is the risk of on‑target/off‑tumor toxicity in normal proliferative tissues?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Neuronal Activity Regulated Mechanisms Promote Growth of Diffuse Hemispheric Glioma H3G34 Mutant
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "we do not see the same effect of BDNF or neuroligin‑3" indicating a gap in knowledge
Speaker said: "we are currently working on phosphoproteomic analyses" showing ongoing uncertainty

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: G34 glioma does not rely on canonical activity‑regulated paracrine factors
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: we do not see the same effect of these proteins in G34

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated synaptic integration and activity‑dependent growth yet concluded that "the tumor type is distinct" without reconciling why shared mechanisms (e.g., BDNF) are absent

key_data_gaps: Identification of receptors mediating N‑CADHERIN and neurofascin effects
In vivo validation of candidate paracrine factors as therapeutic targets

crispro_opportunity.description: Target N‑CADHERIN signaling pathway to block activity‑driven proliferation
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed N‑CADHERIN increased proliferation in G34 cells
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can model downstream signaling networks of N‑CADHERIN and suggest druggable nodes

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine neuronal activity blockers (e.g., perampanel) with N‑CADHERIN inhibition
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker discussed pharmacologic blockade of neuronal activity reducing proliferation
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate combined pharmacologic effects on tumor growth curves

unresolved_questions: What receptors on G34 cells bind N‑CADHERIN and neurofascin?
Can in vivo optogenetic stimulation be translated to pharmacologic modulation in patients?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Targeting MIB and REM4 with REM422 for the Treatment of Pediatric T-Cell Lymphoblastic Leukemia
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "I don't have answers to any of your questions, and it really is intriguing to us that with ALL4, where we would see really nice downregulation of MIB in vivo, it doesn't really care"
Speaker said: "I think it's very specific for MIB" when asked about off‑target effects

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: B‑ALL may be less dependent on MIB despite expression
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: we see that with ALL4, where we would see really nice downregulation of MIB in vivo, it doesn't really care
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: some T‑ALL PDXs achieved complete responses

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: No biomarker‑driven selection of MIB‑high versus low PDXs
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Single mouse per arm limits statistical power

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Speaker noted it is already in clinical trials for AML, suggesting limited exclusive IP for pediatric use
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Single‑agent approach may be insufficient for heterogeneous ALL subtypes

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed robust MIB protein knockdown ex vivo but concluded that "some T‑ALLs are not dependent on MIB" without providing mechanistic explanation

key_data_gaps: Correlation of MIB expression levels with response not fully explored
Long‑term durability of remission after treatment cessation

crispro_opportunity.description: Create a predictive MIB dependency signature to select T‑ALL patients for REM422 therapy
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted variable responses despite similar MIB knockdown
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate expression, copy‑number, and dependency data to generate a MIB‑dependency score

crispro_opportunity.description: Combine REM422 with agents targeting parallel pathways (e.g., NOTCH inhibitors) to overcome resistance in B‑ALL
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted limited efficacy in B‑ALL despite MIB knockdown
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate synthetic lethal interactions with MIB suppression

unresolved_questions: What molecular features distinguish responsive from non‑responsive T‑ALL PDXs?
Does prolonged REM422 dosing lead to resistance via alternative splicing mechanisms?
